one_line_takeaway: iOS apps leak internal CocoaPods dependency names/versions through their bundled Framework directories and Info.plist metadata, making 22.62% of a 9,212-app dataset potentially exploitable for dependency-confusion RCE and 1.76% (162 apps) vulnerable to dependency hijacking via abandoned domains/GitHub namespaces or CocoaPods' notification-free ownership-change design -- the first systematic study of these attack vectors on the iOS software supply chain.
key_finding: 22.62% of apps (2,084 of 9,212) exposed at least one unregistered CocoaPod dependency name/version (9,866 vulnerable framework instances), i.e., potentially susceptible to dependency confusion; the PoC succeeded in achieving demonstrable installs against 9 of 33 tested companies (three rated critical, three high, one medium by the recipients). Separately, 162 apps (1.76%) used dependencies vulnerable to hijacking attacks (97 apps / 1.05% via 213 CocoaPods libraries with owner accounts tied to abandoned email domains -- most prevalently DZNEmptyDataSet, used by 63 apps -- plus 80 apps / 0.87% via 5,056 abandoned GitHub URLs among 83,632 checked, including three apps with over 100 million installs). On GitHub itself, 834 public repositories used vulnerable dependencies (671 via abandoned CocoaPods GitHub URLs, 121 via abandoned owner domains, 43 via SwiftPM, 11 via Carthage), including a repo with 7,129 stars. Only 1.36% of CocoaPods libraries specified any integrity check (commit hash or archive hash) in any version, and only 0.79% did so consistently across all versions. For Go, of 4,520 GitHub namespaces and 219 domains extracted from 5,848 go.mod files, 30 namespaces (0.66%) and 1 domain (0.46%) were available; manual verification confirmed 7 of the available namespaces (23.33%) were genuinely hijackable, affecting popular projects such as cubefs (5,175 stars) and aws/amazon-ecs-agent. For npm-in-iOS, 79 of 671 extracted package names (from 808 apps) were unregistered, affecting 27 distinct apps (one with over 50 million installs), with no abandoned-email-domain cases found.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of software supply chain attacks against iOS apps' dependency management systems. The authors show that iOS app bundles (via the Frameworks directory and embedded Info.plist metadata) leak internal CocoaPods dependency names and versions, enabling dependency confusion attacks that can achieve remote code execution (RCE) on developer machines and build servers through CocoaPods' prepare_command mechanism. They also identify dependency hijacking vectors stemming from CocoaPods' passwordless, silent-ownership-change authentication model, from abandoned owner-email domains, and from abandoned GitHub namespaces/domains used to host dependency source code (affecting CocoaPods, Carthage, and SwiftPM). They quantify exposure across a dataset of 9,212 iOS apps and matched GitHub repositories, practically demonstrate dependency confusion RCE against apps from nine companies via responsible-disclosure programs, and extend the analysis to five additional ecosystems (Cargo, Go modules, Maven, npm, pip) to compare authentication/hosting/code-execution defenses and derive mitigation recommendations.
threat_model: Man-At-The-End (MATE) attacker who can reverse-engineer publicly distributed iOS app binaries to extract internal dependency names/versions (via Frameworks/*.framework directory names and Info.plist CFBundleVersion/CFBundleIdentifier fields), then (1) register a same-named, same-versioned malicious package in the public CocoaPods repository to win dependency resolution when a misconfigured Podfile mixes public and private sources and a developer runs `pod update`, achieving RCE on the developer's machine or CI/build server via CocoaPods' prepare_command arbitrary shell execution; or (2) take over a dependency's hosting location by re-registering an abandoned custom domain or an abandoned/renamed GitHub namespace+repo-name combination that CocoaPods, Carthage, or SwiftPM still resolve to (via GitHub's URL-forwarding-after-rename behavior), thereby injecting malicious code into the dependency itself; or (3) hijack a CocoaPods library owner account by taking over an abandoned email domain used for CocoaPods' passwordless email-confirmation login, silently adding/removing co-owners (no notification is sent for ownership changes) and publishing malicious versions.
limitations: The authors state their measurement provides only a lower bound: apps that embed CocoaPods dependencies without the use_frameworks! flag (statically linked into the binary rather than placed in the Frameworks directory) are missed entirely, and .bundle-directory-based detection was dropped for being false-positive prone. They did not test real-world dependency hijacking against production libraries (only handcrafted toy examples), citing the risk of breaking build processes for many downstream apps; hijacking demonstrations were confined to a legally/ethically scoped PoC on apps with active responsible-disclosure programs. They explicitly excluded typosquatting/social-engineering attacks and did not attempt to detect malicious/outdated packages already present in these ecosystems, treating both as out of scope and future work. No longitudinal data was collected (a single snapshot), and the Maven hijacking hypothesis was not empirically tested due to ethical concerns about DNS/domain-ownership verification. The dependency-confusion PoC ran for only a two-week window, which the authors note likely caused an undercount since CocoaPods only re-resolves on `pod update`.

datasets_used: 2024 dataset: 9,212 iOS apps (with matched Android versions), from Schmidt et al. prior work
disclosure_2025: 279 iOS apps from 105 companies with responsible-disclosure/bug-bounty programs (HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Intigriti, plus Microsoft/Google)
CocoaPods Specs repository: 99,261 CocoaPods libraries (public specification data pulled from GitHub)
5,848 go.mod files from 2,811 GitHub repositories (>1,000 stars, 2,076 namespaces) collected in March 2025
iOS app bundles scanned for node_modules/ and www/plugins/ paths to extract npm package names
quantitative_results: 2,084 apps (22.62%) potentially vulnerable to dependency confusion (9,866 unregistered frameworks)
Dependency-confusion PoC succeeded against 9 of 33 tested companies (3 critical, 3 high, 1 medium severity classifications)
162 apps (1.76%) used dependencies vulnerable to hijacking attacks overall
97 apps (1.05%) vulnerable via 213 CocoaPods libraries tied to 107 abandoned owner-email domains (out of 8,243 owner domains checked); DZNEmptyDataSet used by 63 apps was the most common
80 apps (0.87%) vulnerable via 5,056 abandoned GitHub URLs (out of 83,632 checked), including 3 apps with >100M installs
83 abandoned source domains (4.22% of 1,969 checked), affecting 127 libraries; only 1 of 25 vulnerable-hosted libraries had any integrity check, and it did not cover the vulnerable version
Only 1.36% of 99,261 CocoaPods libraries specified an integrity check (commit/archive hash) in any version; 0.79% did so in all versions
834 GitHub repositories used vulnerable dependencies (671 CocoaPods-GitHub, 121 CocoaPods-owner, 4 CocoaPods-source, 43 SwiftPM, 11 Carthage); average star count 42.25, max 7,129
Of GitHub-hijackable URLs with >=100 stars (236 checked), 186 (78.81%) were actually registerable; 42 vulnerable repos had >1,000 stars despite GitHub's 5,000-download retirement threshold

Go: 4,520 GitHub namespaces / 219 domains extracted; 30 namespaces (0.66%) and 1 domain (0.46%) nominally available; manual verification confirmed 7 namespaces (23.33% of available) genuinely hijackable, spanning 9 image-names and affecting projects like cubefs (5,175 stars) and aws/amazon-ecs-agent
npm-in-iOS: 671 npm package names extracted from 808 apps (8.77%); 79 unregistered names across 27 apps, one app with >50M installs; no abandoned-email-domain cases found
154 disclosure emails sent to developers of apps using vulnerable dependencies
baselines_compared: Cargo (crates.io): GitHub-bound authentication (2FA-enforced since March 2023), direct server-side hosting (not URL forwarding), no install-time code execution -- immune to the RCE-via-install vector

Go modules: decentralized-looking but effectively centralized via the Go proxy's permanent caching; no built-in authentication (relies on hosting platform, typically GitHub); no install-time code execution, so not vulnerable to classic dependency confusion, but proxy caching can mask/undermine GitHub's namespace-retirement mitigation

npm: mixes public/private dependencies with install-time code execution enabled by default (a `--ignore-scripts` flag exists but is off by default); email-based 2FA delivery is itself vulnerable to abandoned-domain takeover
pip: similar architecture to npm; began enforcing 2FA in 2024 and daily abandoned-domain scanning in 2025 that disables password-reset emails for flagged owners

Maven/Maven Central: requires DNS-based proof of domain ownership for groupId registration; no install-time code execution; hijack risk via abandoned domains not empirically tested for ethical reasons

Prior work Gu et al. (Y. Gu et al., 12 threat categories across 6 registries) found 11,788 hijackable GitHub-hosted Go dependency URLs; this paper extends that by adding domain-abandonment and Go-proxy-caching analysis not covered by Gu et al.
